heading: TONUS QUINTUS — Quomodo Deus sit pater omnium. Caput I
card_summary: This chunk opens the Fifth Tone of De Harmonia Mundi with a chapter on how God — first the eternal Father, then Christ — can be called "father of all." To illuminate this theological claim, Zorzi surveys ancient medical authorities on the origin of semen: Hippocrates attributes it to the brain, Aristotle to the heart, Galen to the liver, and Avicenna synthesizes all three, deriving seed from all three principal members. Zorzi himself resolves the dispute by arguing that each bodily member contributes a portion of seed, so that the generated son bears the image of the father in every part — cor from cor, cerebrum from cerebrum, oculus from oculo — making the physiology of generation a natural simulacrum of divine paternity.
essay:
## Overview and Theological Frame

The seventy-sixth chunk inaugurates the *Tonus Quintus* of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* with a programmatic chapter headed *Quomodo Deus sit pater omnium* ("How God is the father of all," Caput I, pp. 1644–1645). The chapter's declared goal is to explain in what sense first the eternal Father and then Christ can properly be called *pater omnium*. Rather than opening with scripture or Kabbalistic authority, Zorzi deploys a characteristically syncretic move: he grounds the theological claim in natural philosophy, reading corporeal generation as a *simulacrum* and *imago* of divine generation. The argument rests on a principle he states explicitly — that in inferior things there are shadows and images of divine realities (*in his inferioribus sint simulachra, et imagines rerum divinarum*) — a thoroughly Neoplatonic axiom that runs throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*.

## The Medical Doxography: Hippocrates, Aristotle, Galen, Avicenna

The vehicle for the analogy is a survey of ancient and medieval medical opinion on the *origo seminis*. Zorzi enumerates four positions:

1. **Hippocrates** — the frothy and fleshy part of seed (*pars spumosa et carnosa*) derives from the brain.
2. **Aristotle** — seed derives from the heart (*a corde*), as the primary seat of the soul.
3. **Galen** — seed derives from the liver (*ab epate*), where the natural, digestive, and generative faculty resides.
4. **Avicenna** — synthesising the three preceding views, seed flows from all three principal members: heart, brain, and liver (*a tribus membris principalibus, a corde videlicet, cerebro, et hepate*).

> *Existimavit itaque Hippocrates partem spumosam, et carnosam seminis emanare a cerebro, Aristoteles a corde, quod est praecipua animae sedes, Galenus ab epate, cum in eo virtus naturalis digestiva, et generativa situata sit, sed Avicena a tribus membris principalibus, a corde videlicet, cerebro, et hepate, ut omnium admittat sententias, et rationes, effluere profitetur.*

<cite>p. 1644</cite>

Zorzi notes a further view — attributed to Aristotle's record of *aliqui* — that seed is drawn from every member of the body, justified by three observations: pleasure during generation is felt throughout the body; any scar on a father's limb produces a similar mark on the offspring; and every part of the child is shaped in the image of the father.

## Zorzi's Own Resolution

Zorzi distances himself from those who attribute the patterning of the child to the father's imagination (*forti imaginationi*), dismissing this on two grounds: resemblance occurs without any imaginative act on the part of the generator, and excessive loss of seed weakens each individual member of the generator's body — which proves that each member actually contributes.

> *Dicant igitur alii pro exercitio ingenii, quicquid velint, mihi videtur, quod a quolibet membro decidat portio seminis, unde ex ipso formetur filius cum generantis imagine, quae totum repraesentat, et membra omnia.*

<cite>p. 1644–1645</cite>

His conclusion is emphatic: the son is formed from the father's image which represents the whole and all members. The parallelism is then made explicit — heart from heart, brain from brain, eye from eye, hand from hand:

> *Et si cor geniti est a corde generantis, cerebrum a cerebro, oculus ab oculo, manus a manu, et sic de unoquoque, esto quod virtus, et spiritus praecipue sit a cerebro, corde, et epate.*

<cite>p. 1645</cite>

## Cosmological and Theological Significance

This medical excursus is not a digression; it is foundational to Zorzi's argument about divine paternity. The logic is analogical and Neoplatonic: just as in natural generation every part of the father is communicated to the son so that the son bears the father's image wholly and in every particular, so too the eternal Father communicates himself entirely to the Son. The *simulachra et imagines rerum divinarum* in lower nature serve as the entry point for understanding relations within the Godhead and, by extension, the relationship of God as creator-father to all creatures.

The choice of Avicenna as a synthesising authority alongside Hippocrates, Aristotle, and Galen is characteristic of Zorzi's inclusive humanist method, welcoming Arabic-Aristotelian medicine into a chain of natural wisdom that points upward toward theological truth. The mention of *spiritus* as principally deriving from brain, heart, and liver also anticipates Zorzi's broader pneumatology, in which the tripartite division of bodily spirits (*natural, vital, animal*) mirrors higher triadic structures.

## Place in the Tonus Quintus

As the opening chapter of the Fifth Tone, this section sets the exegetical and philosophical register for what follows: a sustained meditation on divine fatherhood as it ramifies through the cosmos, with natural philosophy providing the analogical scaffolding that Zorzi will then fill out with Scripture, Kabbalah, and Neoplatonic authority in subsequent chapters.
